title: About
text: Li Tang is a bioinformatics postdoctoral researcher working in computational genomics, with research interests spanning mitochondrial genomics, variant interpretation, sequencing-data analysis, nuclear genome regulation, and 3D genome architecture.

title: Research
text: My research centers on computational genomics approaches for understanding how genome regulation, genetic variation, and genome organization influence cellular function and human disease. I develop and apply AI, deep-learning, and large-scale genomic methods across two complementary areas: nuclear genome regulation and 3D genome architecture, and mitochondrial genetics and biology.

title: Research
section: Nuclear Genome Regulation and 3D Genome Architecture
text:
Nuclear Genome Regulation and 3D Genome Architecture

Scientific questions

How do genome sequence and chromatin architecture shape gene regulation?
How do long-range regulatory interactions contribute to cellular identity, development, and disease?
How can computational models connect three-dimensional genome organization with functional genomic readouts?



Computational and AI approaches
This research theme emphasizes machine learning, deep learning, and integrative computational genomics for modeling chromatin folding, regulatory interactions, and gene-expression control. Future analyses may integrate sequence features, chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, and chromosome-conformation data when verified datasets are available.


Representative projects

Modeling three-dimensional genome organization and chromatin architecture.
Investigating links between long-range regulatory interactions and gene-expression programs.
Developing interpretable computational approaches for regulatory genomics.




Future directions
Future work will develop deep-learning and AI-driven frameworks that connect genome sequence, chromatin organization, and regulatory function, with careful attention to interpretability and disease relevance.

title: Research
text:
Mitochondrial Genetics and Biology

Scientific questions

How does mitochondrial DNA variation influence mitochondrial function, evolution, and human disease?
How can heteroplasmy and NUMT-related artifacts be distinguished in large-scale sequencing data?
How can population-scale genomics improve mitochondrial variant interpretation?



Computational and AI approaches
This research theme combines artificial intelligence, population-scale genomics, evolutionary analysis, and sequencing-data workflows to study mtDNA variation. Existing repository content identifies mitochondrial DNA variation, variant interpretation, NUMTs, heteroplasmy, comparative genomics, and WGS/WES analysis as current areas of focus.


Representative projects

Characterizing mitochondrial DNA variation and heteroplasmy in sequencing data.
Improving interpretation of mitochondrial variants with computational and AI-based approaches.
Studying NUMTs and other artifacts that affect mitochondrial variant analysis.
Applying comparative and population genomics to mitochondrial evolution and disease relevance.




Future directions
Future work will refine computational frameworks for mitochondrial variant interpretation, integrate population and evolutionary context, and improve robust analysis of mtDNA variation in human genomic datasets.

title: Research
section: Connecting Nuclear and Mitochondrial Genome Biology
text:
Connecting Nuclear and Mitochondrial Genome Biology
Future research may investigate how nuclear genome regulation and mitochondrial genetic variation jointly influence cellular phenotypes. This direction is framed as a forward-looking research goal: the aim is to build computational frameworks that connect complementary layers of genome biology without overstating experimentally established links.
